speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number four. Today's date is August the 16th, 1996. The survivor's name is Jack Gun. So you were talking about liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing the Russian soldiers come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? What-- What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saw quite a few Russian soldiers, some on horses, some on motorcycles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you at the time when they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were right on Mr. Preemus's. And like I said, there wasn't that many, but it was like a-- a deserted little village. So there was a few. And naturally, they came in, you know, more into the big cities.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after they came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, soon as we saw the Russian soldiers, we-- we left Mr. Preemus and went back into our-- to Rozyszcze, which wasn't that far away. A distance of maybe 20 kilometers, something like that. And we went right into our house. The house was empty. I believe it was occupied by the-- by German soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there came a lot of more people from our town that survived, came out of the woods, or some in hiding by some gentiles. And a lot of them came right into our house, because our house was quite huge. And it was in good shape. Some of the other ones were demolished. Some were bad, you know? So we had quite a nice home. So lot of the survivors, maybe eight, 10 all lived in our house. And we were searching, looking, hoping, maybe-- maybe our parents will show up. Maybe an aunt, uncle, cousin. Unfortunately, nobody showed. And when-- when exactly was this, the liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately sometimes in March, 1944, which was quite-- quite early. Because the war wouldn't end till 19-- May of 1945. But we were so close to the-- Russia, that this is when they came in to our town. And the front was still going on. In fact, I don't know if it was three weeks, four weeks, six weeks, some-- a short period when we were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard a lot of shooting going on, and we asked some of the Russian soldiers, what's-- what's happening? They said, nothing. They says, we're-- it's a Russian holiday, you know, they're shooting up in the air. So we didn't pay any attention. Then this one day, I was-- I was sitting in the outside bathroom-- we still had outside bathrooms-- and all of a sudden, my brother ran out and-- after me, called me. And he say, come on, come on. I says, what's going on? Where are we going?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, we got to run. The Germans were coming back in to our town. They took back over. And we got on army trucks and we ran with the Russian soldiers. We crossed the bridge, and as we crossed the bridge, they bombed the bridge. And then we went on to another town by the name of [? Kiverts, ?] which was not too far, and boarded a train, and went into Rowne, which was 150 kilometers away from Rozyszcze. And we landed there, and there was still-- that was closer towards Kiev in Russia. And we stayed there for about maybe six weeks. And we stayed at some kind of refugee house, where there was a lot of, you know, displaced people from all over. And we didn't know anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no food. We used to go around begging for food. We-- I remember we went into a-- to a older couple. They were, I believe, Seven Day evangelists. You know, they believe in the Sabbath was their holy day?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we told them, you know, we're survivors and we just lived through the war, and we have nothing to eat. So they-- they were old, and they needed some help. We used to cut wood for them. My brother and I used to cut with a saw. We sawed wood I guess for their oven, and they used to give us the dry-- dried-out bread. And this is how we stayed. This is what we were living on for about-- probably about six weeks. And then we heard that the Russians came back into Rozyszcze. So we came into Rozyszcze. And my brother got very sick with typhoid fever, and they took him to an army hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to come to visit him, and he looked like a skeleton. And then after he was in the hospital for about two weeks or so, three weeks, they-- he was gettin' better, you know, was ready to come out. They said-- they said he has to join the army, you know? Because we still needed people in the army. We're still fighting. So I started to cry, and I still-- I told them, I said, what do you mean, you're taking him to the army? Says I'm-- he's my-- that's all I have. I says, he's my father. He's my mother. He's everything, I said. I cannot be without him. And I had to believe I made a couple of trips there, back and forth, and back and forth, and somehow, they let him out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also remember myself getting very sick with typhoid and laying in our house. And-- But the army doctor came and see me, and he wanted to take me to hospital. And I-- for some reason, I was afraid to go or whatever. I was kicking him so badly where I didn't go. I said, leave me alone or whatever, and they gave me medication or shots. And somehow, I got better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any-- any kind of diseases that you or your brother had during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war? No. We were never sick. Never got any colds. I'm starting to believe the doctors now, that they say you don't get colds from being in the cold. We never had a cold, and we were out many, many days and nights where it was below freezing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the lice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- was it predominantly the lice that you had when you were in the forest? Or?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we had much more when we were in that bunker. Because I guess there was no air hitting us, you know? Had no fresh air, enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is where it was predominantly more the lice, was in the bunker. But I believe, in the forest, it wasn't as bad. You know, we were more in the open, where we could shake them off and all, you know? So after you had typhus, you-- all right. What-- How-- How long did it take till it went away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably a week, 10 days, something like that. And I got better. And then, all of a sudden, they-- they came to the house and gave my brother a notice. This was that they want to see him in the-- in the draft board. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were still drafting people.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians, yeah. He was in-- He was 20 years old, just about. Not quite. And they were drafting, I'm sure, from 18 and on, or 17, and they still needed a lot of soldiers because the war was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he got the notice, he went down there, and said, you know, I mean, you're of age. and we need you in the Red Army. So my brother said, you know, he says I survived with a little brother. He's not quite 10. What am I going to do with him? Who's gonna take care of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, don't worry. He says, we'll-- we'll take care of him, or we'll send him to a home in Russia, or we're gonna-- We'll do something. He says, But you, we need, and we need you immediately. He says, well, he says, immediately. He says, give me 24 hours at least. He says, let me go home, talk to my brother, and explain to him, and, you know, get something straightened out. OK, they say. We'll give you 24 hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: You'll report tomorrow, this and this time. [SIGHS] So when he came back, he told me. He say, you know, said, they want me in the Russian army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he's-- So he says to me, but I'm not going. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, what do you mean, you're not going?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I'm not going. So he says, I'm taking off. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he did. He says, I will let you know somehow where I am, and-- and we had a woman in our house that was living. You know, I told you there was about 10, 12 people living in our house. And one of them was a woman from our town. She was an elderly woman, and she was in the 60s, probably. And my brother told her, he says, please, you take car of my brother while I'm gone. See so he gets fed and whatever. And he took off. And I didn't hear from him for I don't know how long, a few weeks. And then somebody came and told me. He sent somebody to-- to tell me where he's at. He ran away to the city called [INAUDIBLE] [? Wolensk, ?] which also maybe a 100, 150 miles away, or kilometers. And he went there, and he came there and registered under a different name, and called himself [? Myatek ?] [? Zimerin. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And got a job working as a assistant bookkeeper or something, in one of the offices, and used to get, you know, cards for meals, and he stayed there. And I was in Rozyszcze. In the meantime, the KGB used to come wake me up at night, and take me for questioning. Where is your brother? You must know where he is?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, he was a deserter. And I-- Well, probably maybe the first time they questioned me, I didn't even know. And then the other couple times that they questioned me, I knew already. But naturally, I was smart enough not to tell 'em. And then they always did this at night time, you know, to disorient you. And-- But they couldn't break me down to tell where he is. And they used to threaten, we're going to send you to Russia to a orphanage, and this, and that. And I didn't respond, and they let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long would they keep you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couple of hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that go on for? How many times did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd say about three times. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then while I was there alone in Rozyszcze, I-- I became a black marketeer. I-- Another kid, older than me, maybe about 17, he had a Russian uniform. There was a big shortage of yeast in that town, you know, to bake bread and rolls, and that was a very, very-- a demanding commodity. And so, he-- he was dressed in a Russian uniform, and he got me a Russian uniform. You know, they had lot of young kids in the Russian army. I was skinny, but I was tall. We both went on a train. Went into-- into Rowne and bought yeast. And came back and sold it. And that's the first-- at the-- at the age of 10, I became a merchant on the black market. And the-- the people used to, you know, make rolls and used to go to the train station just to sell them for the people, the soldiers, and people traveling and over, and over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long was Anszel gone for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Without me? Probably three to four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about him a lot during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was thinking about him. Naturally, I missed him. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a lonely feeling, but, you know, as I got used to everything else, I got used to that. I was-- I was already used to-- getting used to bad things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very adaptable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. Adaptable to loneliness, and to all kinds of circumstances or situations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when your brother came back? How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did not come back. One day, I was standing outside. I was watching-- the Russians were having a parade, marching. I was very enthused about that. I was standing outside and this I recall very vividly. And a young lady came over and tapped me on the shoulder. And she says, are you-- somebody must have pointed me out to her-- says, are you [? Yankele ?] Gun?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, yes. She says, come on. I got to talk to you. I says, yes? Who are you? And so, she said-- She introduced her self, her name, I don't remember her name. She says, your brother, Anszel, [? sent ?] after you. And tonight at whatever, 6:00, 7:00, 8:00 o'clock, we're taking a bus. So we're going to [INAUDIBLE] [? Wolensk, ?] That's where your brother is. I says, OK, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those days, you weren't afraid of getting kidnapped as today. And she told me that, so I-- I believed her. I figure she knows, and otherwise, she wouldn't be there. And she took me on a bus and we came to [INAUDIBLE] [? Wolensk, ?] and I met up back with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like, the reunion?

speaker: interviewee
text: The reunion? I mean, it was emotional, naturally. We kissed. I mean, I-- I'd even kiss my brother today. So that was nothing special, you know? And I was very happy to be reunited, naturally. And we stayed there, and he used to take me with him when he-- I-- He had, like, a little room someplace that I slept with him. And whenever he had-- we want to eat dinner, he used to-- he used to have these cards or points, whatever you call them, to go to the restaurant to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't much eating, you know? Some kind of soups, or hot soup and some-- and some mush, or farina, whatever. And this is how we lived there. But he got very frightened there.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day he was walking on the street and he saw a Ukrainian fellow about his age that he knew from Rozyszcze. And he was scared, you know, that, hey, he might-- he might go back and report him. And you know, being a deserter, you know, the penalty is, they kill you. So at that time, from-- you see, [INAUDIBLE] [? Wolensk ?] was very close already to the Polish border. It was very close to Chelm. And they were-- you know, they were-- people that-- they were Polish citizens were allowed to go back to Poland. And what they were doing, taking people to Poland, and then the-- some Ukrainians that were in Poland were coming back to the Ukraine. And so-- so my brother wanted, naturally, to get out of the Russian dominance. He shouldn't be recognized, he shouldn't be seen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we started to try to get over to Poland. And they were-- you know, had to make out papers and apply, just-- and that all took a little time. So in the meantime, we thought maybe we can smuggle the border illegally. So he-- he-- there were Russian convoys of trucks going across from-- from [INAUDIBLE] [? Wolensk ?] to Chelm. So he got ahold of a Russian driver and told them, he says, you know, how about taking my brother? And then another friend of ours had a-- also had a-- was with a sister younger than him. So he said, how about taking these two people across? And they bought him a-- a couple bottles of vodka. And a Russian soldier at that would do anything for a bottle of vodka. And we got underneath the benches, you know, in a army truck. You know, they were covered with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, what do you call it? Never can remember the name. Canvas?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a green canvas. And we got under each-- under each bench. And as our luck would have it, as soon as we got to the border there, they opened the-- opened the canvas, and here is a little boy and an older girl lying under each-- under each bench. So naturally, they took us off, took us for questioning.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are you doing? Why are you smuggling the borders? So I told them, I says, look, I says, I just got over the war here, and got out from the Germans. And I have an aunt living in Lublin and I'd like to get over to Poland. He says, all right, well, why do you do it this way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I don't know. I saw trucks. They told me they were going to Poland. I figured I can get on them. Anyway, they took me and this lady, put us in jail, each one in a different cell. And the Russian technique, again, at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: They know at  night is a good time to question [LAUGHS] people. And they took me and interrogating a 10-year-old? Why? But the 10-year-old was already quite much older in-- in the sense that a 10-year-old, you know, had enough knowledge to tell them and not to buckle down-- you know, not to tell them what they want to hear. And-- And I kept telling the same story, and the same story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kept me there, I think, two nights, and they let us out. Somehow, my brother found out about it. I don't know how, but he knew that they-- they held up the trucks and they took us away. And they let us out, and my-- we went back. My brother saw. He says I heard all about it. And then he went back to this committee that was making out the papers. And you know, you-- luck played a lot-- you know, a lot with us. And there, on the board, when we-- there were Polish people, sitting on the board, making out the papers. And they ask you your name. Naturally, I said, Gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where you from in Poland? Rozyszcze. He says, Gun? He says, you know what? He says, I knew your father. I used to deal with him. He says, what happened to your father? What happened? I said, most likely, he got killed. And my brother told him the whole story.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, look, he says, I have a 10-year-old myself. I survived the war, and they wanted to take me in the Russian army, and I wasn't about to leave my brother alone after saving him from the Nazis. He says, you got to do something to get us over into Poland as fast as you can. And he did this for us, and we didn't have to wait too much longer, maybe a month or so, and we got over to Poland. And when we got over to Poland, we already-- my brother met my present sister-in-law. She is from this town, [INAUDIBLE] [? Wolensk. ?] And she traveled with us, and we get into Poland. And we were a short time in Lublin. We were also in Gdansk, which was Danzig, and Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said your brother met his wife there? Your sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. His-- His present wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my present sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they weren't married. But you know, they liked each other, I guess, and they traveled together. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And so where did you go after that? Where did you end up in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- We were in Poland in different places for-- my-- my brother was doing a little black marketing. And we were probably in Poland, I don't know, this-- we probably got over there-- [SIGHS] let's see-- I lost the span of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so before you were-- you were there about a year in Poland, till about '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we got into Poland probably at the end-- the way-- the-- the end of '44, I would say, maybe December, maybe January, '45 already, you know? I don't know exactly, but within that time span. And in Poland, like I said, we were-- my brother tried to do a little black marketing, and going from one town to the other. And my present sister-in-law sort of-- she wasn't my sister-in-law, but she showed a lot of compassion for me. She's a very compassionate woman, and tried to show some-- you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: She knew I-- She realized, you know, I'm 10 years old and-- and I lost my parents at eight. And you know, she was very, very sympathetic to me. And a lot of times, maybe when my brother use to holler, she would say, don't holler at him, or whatever, you know? Tell me something. When you went back to Rozyszcze, did you have-- were there any valuables that your parents had hid before you went to the ghetto that-- that you were able to recover?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only thing my brother did recover is some pictures in the attic of our old-- of our house. And also some addresses of family that we had in the United States, which was very, very helpful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you said you ended up in a DP camp? You were in a DP camp for a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- we're getting ahead of the story a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Poland, and in Poland, as we-- the longer we stayed there in Poland, there started to be a lot anti-Semitism. It never-- never left Poland. And we heard of instances where Jews were killed. And later on as-- after we'd left already, I'm sure you heard of the Kielce pogrom, where, in 1946, where quite a few Jews were killed right on the road. And as was starting already while we were there in '45. And we decided-- well, we-- my brother decided that it's time to get out of Poland also, that this is no haven for Jews. And the best thing is for us to leave. So he got involved with this organization that was operating in Poland called Bricha, which was taking Jews out of Poland towards the west, to Germany, to Austria, to Italy, with the idea of bringing him over to Israel illegally. And we started to work on that, and we-- we-- they took us and gave instructions, what to do, you know, how to act, how to-- when they cross the border. We weren't allowed to speak anything unless we knew Hebrew, because Hebrew-- we were supposed to travel as Greeks, repatriated Greeks, back to Greece. So we traveled on trains, and buses, or trucks as Greeks. We use to wear these round berets that they wear. And we-- When-- We were in Czechoslovakia and Bratislava for a few days. And wound up in Vienna, Austria at Rothschild's-- what's called the Rothschild's Hospital or Rehabilitation Center. And from there, they send you to different places. And we wound up, us, by coincidence, I guess they sent to Linz, Austria, and there, we were in a DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately two years, a little bit longer maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kinds of things went on?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the DP camp? Oh, in DP camp, was already a paradise. We received the packages from the UNRRA-- United Nations Rehabilitation. And that's when I got my first schooling. I just started school there at an improvised school. And we had plenty of food, and things were quite well--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what'd you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --from what we came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your brother do in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother worked in a-- the Jewish-- they called it a Jewish [INAUDIBLE], which was run by Mr. Wiesenthal, by Simon Wiesenthal, the one that was hunting all the Nazis. So my brother worked for him, and I was going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did he do for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clerical work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I met him.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the DP camp. But he lived outside the DP camp, and had an office outside of the DP camp, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, until when were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the DP camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there till November of '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there? From there, we started our journey towards the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape number four.
